title: Abi Jaoudi & Azar Trading Corp. v Monrovia Tobacco Corp. [1988] LRSC 33; 35 LLR 22 (1988) (29 July 1988)
text:
Abi Jaoudi & Azar Trading Corp. v Monrovia Tobacco Corp. [1988] LRSC 33; 35 LLR 22 (1988) (29 July 1988)
THE ABI JAOUDI & AZAR TRADING CORPORATION
, by and
thru its President, ELIE J. ABI JAOUDI, Appellant, v.
THE MONROVIA TOBACCO
CORPORATION
, by and through its Managing Director, A. D. A. M'CORMACK,
Appellee.
Heard: April 25 & 26, 1988. Decided: July 29, 1988.
1. The circuit court, being a court within the judiciary with general
jurisdiction over all disputes between parties-litigants seeking
judicial
determination thereof, has jurisdiction over matters in which the subject
involves an alleged breach of contract.
2. Except for subject matter jurisdiction, all other jurisdictional issues
raised as grounds for dismissal of a complaint, must be
made at the time the
moving party files his responsive pleading.
3. Where a motion to dismiss is filed subsequent to the pleading filed by the
moving party, it is prejudicial error for the judge
not to dismiss the motion.
4. A manufacturer does not violate the anti-trust or monopoly provisions of
the General Business Law by appointing another as his
exclusive dealer or
franchisee in a certain territory, to whom alone he will sell his goods, as long
as the competitive products
are readily available to others.
5. Statutory interpretations and application are mandatory and must be
strictly adhered to.
Appellant, Abi-Jaoudi and Azar Trading Corporation, filed an action of
damages for breach of contract in the Sixth Judicial Circuit
Court for
Montserrado County against the Monrovia Tobacco Corporation, appellee. The basis
of the appellant's complaint was that
the appellee had engaged other
distributors to distribute its tobacco products in violation of an agreement
under which appellant
was, with another company, given the exclusive right to
distribute the appellee tobacco products in Liberia. In response to the
complaint,
the appellee filed an answer as well as a motion to dismiss.
Following a hearing on the motion to dismiss, the trial court ruled that the
complaint be dismissed along with the entire action,
thereby sustaining the
defendant's contention that the court lacked jurisdiction over the cause. The
trial court reasoned that under
Article 97 of the Constitution, it was
prohibited from reviewing acts done by the People's Redemption Council
Government, which it
said was responsible for the engagement of more
distributors by the appellee to distribute its tobacco products. From this
ruling,
appellant excepted and announced an appeal to the Supreme Court.
The Supreme Court reversed the ruling of the trial court, holding that the
action was one of damages for breach of contract growing
out of an alleged
breach in 1986, subsequent to the coming into force of the new Constitution. The
Court noted that the action being
one for breach of contract, and the trial
court being one with general jurisdiction over all disputes of that nature, the
lower court
did have the power to hear the case. The Court opined that if the
appellee desired to raise the issue of the jurisdiction of the
court, it should
have done so in compliance with the statute which required that all motions,
except those challenging the subject
matter jurisdiction of the court, should be
filed at the same time as the responsive pleadings are being filed and not
subsequent
to such filing. The appellee, it said, had filed its motion to
dismiss after it had filed its answer.
As to the contention raised in the appellee's answer that the contract was a
violation of the law since it created a monopoly which
was prohibited by law,
the Court made a finding on the factual issue raised in the case, noting that as
a matter of public knowledge,
many brands of cigarettes and other tobacco
products were being imported into Liberia, and that therefore the contract
between the
appellant and appellee could not be said to be one creating a
monopoly which is prohibited under the General Business Law.
The Court therefore reversed the ruling of the trial court and remanded the
case to the trial court to proceed with the disposition
of the law issues.
S. Raymond Horace, Sr.
and
S. Raymond Horace, Jr.
of the Horace
& Horace Law Firm, associated with
Joseph Findley
appeared for
appellant.
Seward M Cooper
and
Philip A. Z. Banks, III,
of the
Tubman Law Firm, in association with
Victor D. Hne
and
S. Edward
Carlor
of the Carlor, Gordon, Hne & Teewia Law Offices appeared for
appellee.
MR JUSTICE JUNIUS delivered the opinion of the Court.
Appellant Abi Jaoudi and Azar Trading Corporation filed an action of damages
for breach of contract before the Sixth Judicial Circuit
Court, Montserrado
County, during its December Term, A. D. 1987, against Appellee Monrovia Tobacco
Corporation. The said complaint
consisted of six (6) counts, with twenty (20)
exhibits made profert therewith. On December 15, 1987, Appellee Monrovia Tobacco
Corporation
filed its answer consisting of twenty-two (22) counts, with 15
exhibits attached thereto. On December 23, 1987, appellant filed its
reply.
Subsequently, on January 2, 1988, the said reply was withdrawn and an amended
reply, consisting of twenty-eight (28) counts
and nine (9) additional exhibits,
was substituted therefor.
Appellee, on January 12, 1988, filed a motion to dismiss appellant's
complaint. The motion to dismiss the complaint was served on
appellant's counsel
on January 13, 1988. Thereafter, a notice of assignment was issued on January
14, 1988, setting January 15, 1988,
at 2 o'clock p.m., as the date and time for
disposition of the said motion.
According to the records certified to us, the notice of assignment was
neither served on appellant itself nor its counsel prior to
the assigned time,
as the sheriffs returns thereon indicates. Upon hearing that the notice of
assignment had been issued, one of
counsel for appellant, in a letter dated
January 15, 1988, addressed to the trial judge then presiding in the court below
requesting
a postponement. Another notice of assignment was therefore issued
rescheduling the case for hearing of the motion to dismiss on January
19, 1988,
at 10 o'clock a.m.
On January 19, 1988, at the call of the case, counsel for appellant requested
a later time to enable them to study and present its
side of the case. This
application was resisted by appellee. The trial judge nevertheless granted the
application and rescheduled
the case for January 20, 1988, at 11 o'clock a.m.
The new assignment was made on the records of the court.
It is noteworthy to mention that when the motion to dismiss appellant's
complaint was served on the appellant, it filed its resistance
on January 19,
1988. As scheduled, the arguments on the motion to dismiss were entertained by
the court
pro et con
from counsel for both parties. Ruling was thereafter
reserved until January 26, 1988. On January 26, 1988, the trial judge delivered
his ruling dismissing appellant's complaint and the entire action without
reservation. It is from this ruling of the trial judge
that appellant excepted
and announced an appeal to this Honourable Court. The appeal thus taken is
before us on a bill of exceptions
containing six (6) counts which we herewith
quote below:
"1. That on the said 26th day of January, A. D. 1988, Your Honour, in
presenting the position ofthe defendant, movant in said motion,
prejudicially
stressed the points raised by defendant in said motion, especially the point of
the Tripartite Agreement, which is
the subject of the action of damages
instituted by plaintiff, without taking into consideration plaintiffs position
on the point
that defendant had pleaded this issue in the motion in a vague and
ambiguous manner because the said defendant had referred to Article
97 (a) of
the Constitution of Liberia which prescribes inquiries into actions taken by the
People's Redemption Council or in its
name pursuant to any of its decrees but
had miserably failed to plead or show in any fashion the existence of any decree
by content,
number time of passage, or any other means, touching on, connected
with, or relevant to the execution of the Tripartite Agreement.
To this
prejudicial part of Your Honour's ruling plaintiff then and there excepted to
the entire ruling.
2. That on the said 26 th day of January, A. D. 1988, when reviewing the
position of the defendant on its motion to dismiss, Your
Honour stated that the
Tripartite Agreement, executed during the reign of the military government
pronouncement and announcements
declaring monopoly illegal, prevented the court
from reviewing or entertaining this action. Plaintiff says that there was no
legal
ground for the statement by the defendant, nor for its apparent acceptance
by Your Honour because neither in defendants motion nor
in the exhibits attached
thereto is there shown any decree of the PRC to this effect and Your Honours
apparent acceptance of this
proposition obviously influenced your ruling to the
prejudice of plaintiff and to which portion of said ruling plaintiff then and
there excepted when it excepted to the entire ruling of Your Honour.
3. That on the said 26th day of January, A. D. 1988, Your Honour in reviewing
the points raised in defendant's motion to dismiss stated
that:
`Defendant maintained further this court cannot legally entertain this action
growing out of the alleged illegal agreement since indeed
we expressly vowed to
uphold the Constitution and statutory laws of this nation. They maintain
therefore that any act performed against
these sacred and supreme laws would, in
reality violate our oath and of course would be equivalent to reviewing the
official act
of the military government.'
Whereas Your Honour made no mention of plaintiff s position in this regard as
revealed in its resistance to the motion to dismiss,
which clearly showed that
the contention of defendant relating to violation of Article 97(a) of the
Constitution could not hold because
said provision of the Constitution limits
its prohibition to executive, legislative, judicial or administrative action,
clearly referring
to governmental actions or actions of persons acting in a
governmental capacity and therefore was inapplicable to this case between
private persons acting in their private capacities, such as defendant's breach
of the tripartite agreement. To this portion of Your
Honour's ruling, plaintiff
then and there excepted when it excepted to the entire ruling.
4. That Your Honour, in passing on the issues purportedly contained in the
motion to dismiss and resistance thereto, said
inter alia:
`Now there are limiting factors inherent in the entire situation, and as we
see it, the test of that relevant constitutional passage
thus compels certain
conditions and resolve (sic). In essence, it required that any executive,
legislative, judicial or administrative
action taken by the People's Redemption
Council or any official, be it military or civilian, in accordance with a decree
of the Council
or in accordance with the policy of government, shall not be
reviewed by any court to the effect of granting relief, issuing order,
and
providing remedy against that action.
FURTHER: Now the crucial question remains whether or not any executive policy
regarding the equal distribution of commercial rights
initiated by the PRC
Government, and/or in fact, does that policy serve as a vehicle for protection
of substantive right as well.
To clarify our position and assemble our thoughts
to explicit understanding, we quote hereunder some paragraphs of the executive
decisions;
Consistent with government's recent pronouncement it is confirmed that the
sole distribution system is indeed abrogated as such venture
are not to the best
interest of the consumer public. March 3, 1982, signed CIC Samuel K. Doe,
Chairman, PRC, and Head of State, R.
L.
That with reference to the above quotation, your Honour apparently overlooked
plaintiffs persistent contention that the position of
defendant, as stated in
its motion, is legally untenable because of inconsistency, ambiguity and
apparent misunderstanding of the
issue, with particular reference to the
quotation of the letter of March 3, 1982, without taking into consideration
plaintiffs exhibit
"B", made profert with its resistance, same being a letter
from CIC Dr. Samuel K. Doe, Head of State and Chairman, PRC, to defendant
dated
March 5, 1984, the last paragraph of which reads:
`As a consequence, you will accordingly restrict the distribution rights for
all tobacco products which your corporation is now marketing
and may wish to
market in Liberia, to the two rightful distributors, namely, the Abi Jaoudi
& Azar Trading Corporation and the
Liberian Distribution Company, with
immediate effect.'
Because of Your Honour's adverse ruling against plaintiff on these points
which plaintiff considered erroneous and prejudicial, it
then and there excepted
to the entire ruling.
5. That Your Honour's ruling on the issue raised in plaintiff's resistance
with respect to defendant's motion to dismiss because it
was not filed in
keeping with the mandatory provision of the statute governing filing of motion
to dismiss, in which Your Honour
stated
inter alia
'Lest we forget, the question requesting the breach of rule of law governing
motions cannot be sanctioned since the question of jurisdiction
can be raised at
any time during trial,'
failed to take into consideration that whether or not the issue is one of
jurisdiction or not the procedure to be followed for filing
of motions must be
adhered to. More importantly, the law relied on by defendant, contained in
[1925] LRSC 7
;
2 LLR
517
(1924), in the case
Hill v. Republic,
decided January 6, 1925, and
[1952] LRSC 23
;
11
LLR 264
(1952), in the case
French Cable Company v. Johnson,
decided
December 12, 1952, respectively, were decided before the coming into effect of
the Liberian Code of Laws Revised in 1973,
which clearly defines when and how
motions to dismiss shall be filed. Your Honour's ruling on this point was
therefore erroneous
and prejudicial since the law therefore is that 'A party
waives any defense enumerated in paragraph 1 of this section which he does
not
present either by motion as hereinbefore provided or in his answer or reply,
except that lack of jurisdiction over the subject
matter is not so waived' and
since the motion was not to the subject matter damaged for the tripartite
agreement - but a thing involved
in the action such as an alleged PRC decree on
the question of monopoly and distributorship which we insist is nonexistent. To
which
plaintiff then and there excepted when it excepted to the entire ruling.
6. That Your Honour's entire ruling, dismissing plaintiff's suit including
your review of the positions of the movant/defendant and
the
plaintiff/respondent, as stated in their motion to dismiss and resistance to
same, being erroneous and prejudicial to plaintiff
and not in keeping with the
law controlling, plaintiff then and there excepted, as shown on sheet seven of
the minutes of court for
January 26, 1988."
When this case was called for hearing before us, the appellant presented the
below issues for our consideration:
"(1) That the law extant in this jurisdiction regarding the making of motions
requires that the grounds therefor be stated with particularity
and that the
sole ground set forth in defendant/appellee's motion to dismiss is that the
court below was without power and jurisdiction
to try the instant case. Article
97 (a) of the Constitution of Liberia proscribes judicial inquiries into actions
taken by the People's
Redemption Council or in its name 'pursuant to any of its
decrees'. Defendant/appellee not only failed to plead or otherwise show
the
existence of any PRC decree touching on or connected with the Tripartite
Agreement, but it also totally failed to show the action
of any person acting
pursuant to such an alleged decree tending to abrogate the tripartite agreement
since, in fact, no such PRC
decree had ever existed.
(2) Whether 97 (a) prescribes a judicial inquiry into actions taken by
private persons acting in their private and personal capacity.
(3) That the judge of the court below committed a blatantly prejudicial error
when he relied on and quoted out of context in his ruling
a portion of the
letter addressed to defendant by the then Head of State, dated March 3, 1982, as
evincing an executive policy affecting
the legality of the tripartite agreement
and also that said policy originated from the PRC Government.
(4) That the motion to dismiss the pleading based on jurisdiction as ground
may be made as set forth in count 5 of the bill of exceptions.
That in
connection with the timing of the motion to dismiss, plaintiff/appellant argued
succinctly that it is a legal requirement
that a motion based on the pleadings
in an action must first be made by the party filing the last pleading. The
aforesaid motion
to dismiss was not made until January 12, 1988, although said
reply was the last pleading in the action.
The appellant having rested its argument, appellee presented the below issues
and argued them vehemently before this Court:
(a) Could the court entertain a motion to dismiss for lack of jurisdiction
over the subject matter filed by appellee after the filing
and service of its
responsive pleading?
(b) Can the court entertain an action of damages which grows out of acts done
pursuant to directives of the People's Redemption Council
Government during the
period of military rule in Liberia?
Having given an analysis of the case which is now before us for review, let
us now consider the issues presented by both parties.
The basis of appellee's motion to dismiss the complaint was that the court
below lacked jurisdiction of the subject matter of the
instant cause of action,
in that same was "a subject growing out of acts done and performed by
appropriate government authority during
the period of military rule within the
Republic of Liberia", and that judicial inquiries into these alleged acts of the
military
authority are prohibited by Article 97 (a) of the Constitution of
Liberia. We note that the complaint in the action filed by the
appellant stated
same as being an action of damages for breach of contract. Said complaint is
part of the records transmitted to
us. It unequivocally states that the alleged
breach of contract started in August 1986.
This Court is also convinced, given that the subject matter of the action was
the alleged breach of contract, that the circuit court,
being a court within the
judiciary of the Republic of Liberia, had jurisdiction over the case since it is
a court that has general
jurisdiction over all disputes between parties
litigants seeking a judicial determination thereof.
See
Judiciary Law,
Rev. Code 17:3.2. Thus, it was incumbent on appellee to comply strictly with the
statutory requirement for raising
issues of jurisdiction as grounds for
dismissal of complaints. This requirement is that except for subject matter
jurisdiction, all
other jurisdictional issues, raised as grounds for dismissal
of a complaint, must be made at the time the moving party files his
responsive
pleading.
See
Civil Procedure Law, Rev. Code 1: 11.2(1). We note that
although appellee's answer was served on December 15, 1987, it did not file
its
motion to dismiss until January 12, 1988. As pointed out,
supra,
said
motion did not set out the subject matter which constituted the alleged breach
of contract. The judge of the court below therefore
had no alternative but to
dismiss the aforesaid motion as being untimely, and it was prejudicial error to
have held otherwise.
This Court is constitutionally set up as the final arbiter of all
constitutional issues and is charged with exercising final appellate
jurisdiction in all cases, both as to fact and law. LIB. CONST., art. 65. The
provision referred to states:
"The judicial power of the Republic shall be vested in a Supreme Court and
such subordinate courts as the Legislature may from time
to time establish. The
courts shall apply both statutory and customary laws in accordance with the
standards enacted by the Legislature.
Judgments of the Supreme Court shall be
final and binding and shall not be subject to appeal or review by any other
branch of government.
Nothing in this Article shall prohibit administrative
consideration of the justiciable matter prior to review by a court of competent
jurisdiction."
This constitutional charge is of course with respect to the exercise of the
judicial power of the Republic of Liberia, which the Constitution
exclusively
and expressly grants to the judiciary, with this Court as the normal head
thereof, and such other subordinate courts
as the National Legislature shall
from time to time establish. LIB. CONST. Art. 66. That provision reads as
follows:
"The Supreme Court shall be the final arbiter of constitutional issues and
shall exercise final appellate jurisdiction in all cases
whether emanating from
courts of record, courts not of record, administrative agencies, autonomous
agencies, or any other authority,
both as to law and fact, except cases
involving ambassadors, ministers, or cases in which a county is a party. In all
such cases,
the Supreme Court shall exercise original jurisdiction. The
Legislature shall make no law nor create any exceptions as would deprive
the
Supreme Court of any of the powers granted herein."
We see the statutory provisions with respect to the allowable scope of
enquiry of this Court of matters before it for review and on
appeal and the
manner regarding the disposition of matters on appeal as being in consonance
with the above cited constitutional provisions.
Rev. Code 1: 51 .15 and 55.17.
Our enquiry then is confined to issues raised in the court below and argued in
the briefs by the party-litigants
before us. In disposing of the issues so
raised, this Court is empowered to reverse, affirm or modify judgments before
it, and it
may finally determine such cases or remand same to the court below.
We find authority for this position not only in the last statute
referred to,
but also in this Court's long established exercise of the privilege to set
procedure which should be followed by subordinate
courts in addressing issues in
the proper case. In
Jantzen v. Williams,
[1934] LRSC 35
;
4 LLR 231
(1934), we said:
"This Court has the privilege of settling the procedure of subordinate
courts:"
In
Cummings v. Republic,
[1935] LRSC 9
;
4 LLR 284
(1935), we opined:
"An objection must be sufficiently specific to inform the trial judge of the
point on which a ruling is requested, as well as to avoid
any ambiguity in
presenting the precise question for the consideration of the court of appeal".
In
Yancy v. Republic of Liberia,
[1933] LRSC 14
;
4 LLR 3
(1933), we held as follows:
"The Supreme Court settles the procedure of the courts below."
Thus, under the authority vested in us, we are thereby able to streamline
issues which may be raised by the parties in a case on remand
in the subordinate
courts and, hopefully, avoid several repetitions of issues already solved to
save all concerned both time and
money.
In reviewing appellee's answer, as shown from the records certified from the
court below to this Court, we note that the defense of
impossibility of
performance of contract (force majeure is apparently used synonymous therewith)
is therein set forth. We note also
that this defense is stated as being based on
appellee's allegation that certain acts of the military authority, viz,
declaring the
agreement between the party-litigants, the breach of which is the
basis of the complaint, contrary to the laws of the Republic of
Liberia, and
thus illegal, void and unenforceable. That alleged declaration of illegality and
unenforceability by the military authority,
the appellee says, was subsequently
confirmed by the appropriate civilian authority, namely, the Ministry of
Commerce, following
the institution of the Second Republic on January 6, 1986.
We have already stated,
supra,
the untenability of appellee's contention
of an alleged interference or declaration by the military authority, since same
can have
no relevance to a breach of contract which is clearly stated as
starting in August 1986, after military rule had ended in Liberia.
Not only is the above mentioned defense set out prominently in appellee's
answer, but it is countered at length in appellant's amended
reply. It is also
set forth in the motion to dismiss, countered in the resistance thereto, and
figured significantly in the ruling
of the judge of the court below. Needless to
say, appellant's bill of exceptions addressed the lower courts ruling on this
subject.
Finally, said issue,
pro et con,
was contained in the briefs of
the parties and strenuously argued before this Court. We have therefore read and
heard enough thereon
by the parties and are able to make a definitive decision
with respect thereto.
Similarly entrenched in the records before us in the instant cause and fully
argued by the parties,
pro et con,
is whether or not the contract between
the parties is one that created a monopolistic condition and was therefore in
violation of
the General Business Law, Rev. Code 14: 2.1, enacted in 1973. We
have reviewed the aforesaid contract and note that it designates
the appellant
and another company as the "main and sole distributors" of appellee's tobacco
products in Liberia. These tobacco products
are named as the range of Gold
Dollar Cigarettes and Captain Grant Shag Tobacco. We make the observation, as a
matter of public knowledge,
that in Liberia many brands of cigarettes and other
tobacco products are sold by several importers. We feel comfortable in declaring
today that contracts creating exclusive or sole distributors or dealers of a
manufacturer's or importer's products are not in violation
of the General
Business Law, Rev. Code 14:2.1., "as long as competitive products are readily
available to others." In this decision,
we find it necessary to apply the
reasoning of the United States Supreme Court in several cases in which the
Sherman Act, the first
antitrust statute (the same as the above cited provision
of the General Business Law), was not applied in situations involving exclusive
dealerships. In
54 AM. JUR., 2d.
,
Monopolies, §
87, we find the
following: "A manufacturer does not violate the Sherman Act by appointing
another as his exclusive dealer or franchise
in a certain territory, to whom
alone he will sell his goods, as long as competitive products are readily
available to others. .
. ."
Further, we want to bring to the attention of the lower court that statutory
interpretation and application are mandatory and must
be strictly adhered to.
In reversing the ruling of the judge of the court below, we find ourselves
constrained to remand the instant cause to the said court
for further
proceedings beginning with the disposition of law issues. However, in doing so,
we herewith limit the lower court's authority
and jurisdiction with respect to
the two issues which were raised in the records certified to us and argued by
the parties before
us in order to obviate these issues coming before this Court
again should this case be appealed for a second time.
In view of the foregoing, the ruling of the trial judge dis-missing
appellant's complaint is hereby set aside. The court is hereby
ordered to resume
jurisdiction over the case and proceed from hearing the law issues presented by
the parties, that is, the complaint,
the answer and the reply. Costs in these
proceedings are to abide the final determination. And it is hereby so ordered.
Judgment reversed; case remanded.
MR. CHIEF JUSTICE GBALAZEH
dissents.
I have found it entirely impossible to append my signature to the majority
opinion in this case for several reasons, both as to the
law and the facts of
the matter.
This is an appeal from an adverse decision in the lower court on a motion to
dismiss the complaint in an action of damages for breach
of contract, in which
the appellant alleged that the appellee had defaulted on a Tripartite Agreement
concluded on November 30, 1984.
The plaintiff alleged that the defendant had
agreed to sell all its tobacco products to the plaintiff and another
distributor. The
plaintiff and the other distributor were to be solely
responsible to buy and distribute all over Liberia the tobacco products bought
from the defendant under that agreement, and the defendant was to sell its
products to only the two companies in Liberia.
Subsequently, however, beginning July 1, 1986, the appellee was alleged to
have breached the said Tripartite Agreement when it started
selling its tobacco
products to other distributors. The alleged breach was said to have continued in
spite of several protests from
the appellant. That breach, the appellant
alleged, caused it considerable loss of profits and embarrassment to its
customers who
had relied on its services until the breach. Hence, on December 4,
1987, the appellant brought the action of damages for breach of
contract in the
Civil Law Court for the Sixth Judicial Circuit, Montserrado County.
On December 15, 1987, the appellee filed an answer in which it substantially
denied the allegations set forth in the appellant's complaint,
to the extent
that while there had existed a Tripartite Agreement making the appellant and
another corporation the exclusive distributors
of appellee's goods on the
Liberian market,
yet the overwhelming force of the People's Redemption
Council (PRC) Government
had acted as
force majeure,
rendering
performance of the agreement impossible.
The appellee asserted further that
in any case the Tripartite Agreement amounted to an agreement in restraint of
trade, a monopoly,
and that it was therefore unlawful; that since the agreement
was concluded at the direction of the PRC, it was proscribed by the
1986
Constitution from judicial proceedings of any kind.
The plaintiff filed a
reply on December 23, 1987, but it withdrew same and filed an amended reply on
January 20, 1988. However, on
January 12, 1988, before the filing of the amended
reply, the appellee filed a motion to dismiss the appellant's complaint. The
appellant
appeals here against the granting of the said motion.
The motion was to the effect that the trial court had no jurisdiction over
the subject matter, an action of damages for breach of
a contract that was
substantially engineered by the PRC which had made performance impossible, and
that as a result, in consequence
of Article 97(a) of the 1986 Constitution, the
matter was not to be handled by the courts. The motion concluded that in any
case,
the said contract was an arrangement in restraint of trade, and therefore
a monopoly, contrary to the General Business Law, Rev.
Code 14:2.1. The
appellant resisted the motion, saying that the contract was genuine, legal and
outside the purview of Article 97(a)
of the 1986 Constitution.
After arguments, the judge ruled dismissing the action on the grounds that
the contract was influenced by the overwhelming power of
the PRC, and therefore
proscribed by our Constitution; and further, that to all intents and purposes
the Tripartite Agreement was
in restraint of trade, and therefore a monopoly and
illegal under our laws. The appellant excepted to the ruling and announced an
appeal therefrom, which was thereafter perfected.
In a six-count bill of exceptions, the appellant accused the trial judge of
being bias in favour of the appellee and alleged that
the judge had conducted
the entire trial in appellee's favour. Furthermore, the appellant contended that
the motion to dismiss the
action should have been filed along with the
appellee's answer, and that the Tripartite Agreement did not fall within the
ambit of
Article 97(a) of the 1986 Constitution since the act of the breach
complained of occurred after the period of the PRC, and long after
the
Constitution came into effect on January 6, 1986. The breach, appellant said,
occurred in July, 1986. The above contentions presented
the following issues for
our determination:
1. Can the Civil Law Court for Montserrado County entertain a motion to
dismiss for lack of jurisdiction over a subject matter, which
motion was filed
by a defendant after the filing and service of its answer?
2. Could the trial court entertain an action of damages which grows out of
acts done pursuant to directives of the People's Redemption
Council Government
during the period of military rule in Liberia?
Even restricting myself to the two issues raised by the appellant as my
colleagues have chosen to do without my own acquiescence,
no one can deny that
the Civil Law Court for Montserrado County, a court of record, has jurisdiction
over an "action of damages for
breach of contract, pure and simple;
but where
the said contract is the exclusive creature of the PRC and a proven agreement in
restraint of trade, a monopoly and a legal
nullity, the court has every right
and a duty to refuse jurisdiction over it, by throwing it out.
Certainly, in
the circumstances, a motion brought to dismiss such action of damages deserves
the appellation "motion to dismiss for
lack of jurisdiction over the subject
matter" because it is directed at the grade of the "action of damages", an
action of damages
based on an illegal contract.
As the majority itself accepts, where the motion to dismiss an action is
concerned with the jurisdiction of the court over the subject
matter, it can be
raised at anytime before final judgment. Indeed, the appellee, as defendant, had
filed its motion to dismiss the
action for lack of jurisdiction over the subject
matter before the final judgment. Therefore, it was quite in place, and the
action
was dismissible. (See
Richards v. The Commercial Bank of Liberia,
Inc.,
2OLLR 349 (1971).
See
also
Lamco J. V. Operating Company v.
Verdier,
[1977] LRSC 34
;
26 LLR 180
(1977).
Some other salient considerations have restrained me front joining my
colleagues in their decision and conclusion. Throughout the
pleadings and the
arguments of the briefs before us, the appellee has stressed the overwhelming
weight of the PRC Government over
the Tripartite Agreement of November 30, 1984,
insofar as the PRC had directed its very conclusion, and the extent to which the
PRC
had continued to control the agreement's performance by the appellee.
While the majority of the members of the Court tacitly concede this influence
and direction, they also seek to rationalize it by saying
that only the Judicial
Branch has jurisdiction over a contract and not the Executive Branch; and
further, that the breach of the
said contract had occurred in July 1986, several
months after the 1986 Constitution had come into force, when there was no PRC
Government
at the time. Therefore, they say, the trial court had jurisdiction
over the matter and that Article 97(a) of the Constitution had
no application at
all.
However, my colleagues fail to realize that
the Tripartite Agreement, from
start to finish, was a creature of the PRC, and one can touch no parties of that
Tripartite Agreement
without touching the PRC itself
Apart from the breach,
the Tripartite Agreement itself had the PRC as a base. At the time of the
establishment of the said Tripartite
Agreement on November 30, 1984, there was
already in existence the General Business Law, Rev. Code 14: 2.1 (a & b),
dealing with
monopolies against the restraint of trade. No one who fully
understands the said agreement and the records of this case can deny
that it was
in restraint of trade,
or that the PRC had, throughout the relevant period
heavily influenced both its creation and its performance.
Certainly, without any doubts, the courts have jurisdiction over contractual
obligations in general, but
the particular contract under review can hardly
be discussed with any justice without impeaching the credibility of the PRC
Government,
in violation of Article 97(a) of the 1986 Constitution.
And I
personally refuse to subscribe to any review of the acts of the erstwhile PRC
Government in any measure at all.
Therefore, I am compelled to personally
agree with the ruling of the trial
judge on the subject of PRC involvement in the matter and on the possible
infringement of Article
97(a) by any measure of judicial review of this matter.
Hence, I refuse to side with the ruling of my colleagues on the subject.
Another fundamental point of dissent from the majority concerns the
restrictions in its mandate to the lower court. The mandate itself
is not clear.
The majority of my colleagues have reversed the judgment of the lower court, and
at the same time, in the exercise
of the Court's power to render the judgment
that should have been rendered, they have chosen to rule on the defense of
impossibility
of performance, raised by the appellee. The Court has also ruled
on the issue of the monopolistic nature of the contract by holding
that same is
not in fact in restraint of trade, and therefore legal.
In spite of all these, the mandate directs that the case be remanded to the
trial court for trial of the "action of damages for breach
of contract", and
that same be proceeded with beginning "from ruling on the law issues as
presented by the parties, that is, the
complaint, the answer and the reply."
However, in view of the holding of the majority, it is difficult to see that
there remains
any other issues left for the lower court to decide, since the
said opinion has discussed and ruled on all the material issues raised
by the
pleadings on the merits of the matter. Therefore, there can be no further reason
for remanding the case.
In view of the above, I have refused to append my signature to the judgment.
The Clerk of this Court is therefore hereby instructed
to file this dissenting
opinion as a part of the records in this case.
